title: Quilts of Alternating Sign Matrices
speaker: Sara Billey
abstract:
We present new objects called quilts of alternating sign matrices with respect to two given posets. Quilts generalize several commonly used concepts in mathematics. For example, the rank function on submatrices of a matrix gives rise to a quilt with respect to two Boolean lattices. When the two posets are chains, a quilt is equivalent to an alternating sign matrix and its corresponding corner sum matrix. Such rank functions are used in the definition of Schubert varieties in both the Grassmannian and the complete flag manifold. Quilts also generalize the monotone Boolean functions counted by the Dedekind numbers, which is known to be a #P
-complete problem. Quilts form a distributive lattice with many beautiful properties and contain many classical and well known sublattices, such as the lattice of matroids of a given rank and ground set. While enumerating quilts is hard in general, we prove two major enumerative results, when one of the posets is an antichain and when one of them is a chain. We also give some bounds for the number of quilts when one poset is the Boolean lattice. Several open problems will be given for future development. This talk is based on joint work with Matja\v z Konvalinka.
tags: Workshop on Combinatorics of Enumerative Geometry

proof_stages.label: Rank-function motivation and Boolean growth
proof_stages.summary: The talk extracts common features of ordinary poset ranks, submatrix ranks, and matroid ranks—especially bounded 0/1 growth—and uses Schubert-theoretic motivations to ask for rank-like functions on products of arbitrary ranked posets.

proof_stages.label: Definition and basic model cases of ASM quilts
proof_stages.summary: For finite ranked posets P and Q, an ASM quilt is defined by bottom and top boundary conditions together with Boolean growth along covers, and the chain-by-chain case is identified with corner-sum matrices and alternating sign matrices while arbitrary maximal chains give interwoven ASMs.

proof_stages.label: Alternating sign matrices and their lattice background
proof_stages.summary: The lecture recalls rectangular ASMs, the Mills-Robbins-Rumsey enumeration problem, and the lattice of ASMs extending Bruhat order via corner-sum comparison, motivating an analogous lattice theory for quilts.

proof_stages.label: Distributive quilt lattice and cover criterion
proof_stages.summary: Pointwise order on quilts is shown to give a distributive lattice with pointwise min and max, unique extrema, and a rank function given by the shifted sum of entries; the proof uses an acyclic directed graph whose sinks and sources describe covers, and classical structures such as ASMs, matroids, flag matroids, and spanning-line-configuration orders embed inside.

proof_stages.label: General hardness and antichain-factor enumeration
proof_stages.summary: Counting quilts for arbitrary posets is proved #P-complete by reduction to counting antichains, but for an antichain factor the count is expressed as a sum over convex cutsets, yielding Dedekind-type asymptotics and explicit formulas in the chain-antichain case.

proof_stages.label: Chain-factor enumeration via monotone-triangle data
proof_stages.summary: For quilts of type P by C_n, jump sets form interlacing monotone-triangle data, giving eventual polynomiality in n with coefficients counted by fundamental quilts and leading term counted by standard quilts; when P is a chain this recovers shifted staircase tableaux and yields asymptotics for rectangular ASMs.

proof_stages.label: Boolean factors and open geometric realizability problems
proof_stages.summary: Boolean-factor counts are bounded by Dedekind-number asymptotics, and the closing agenda asks for criteria for quilts to be realized by matrices or flag matrices and for analogues of ASM varieties, polytopes, descents, symmetries, random limits, and finite-field counts.
